questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of inserting at the end of an ArrayList in the average case?

options.text: O(1) — appending to the end of an array takes constant time
O(n) — the array must be scanned to find the end position
O(n²) — nested loops are required for each insertion
O(log n) — binary search is needed to find the insertion point

questions.explanation: ArrayList's internal array is indexed — accessing arr[size] is O(1). When capacity is available, append is a simple write + increment: O(1). The O(n) cost comes only when the internal array is full and must be resized (typically 1.5×), which happens infrequently enough that amortized append remains O(1). The confusion often comes from LinkedList — appending to a LinkedList is also O(1) but has higher constant factors due to node allocation. Option B describes a naive list scan, not ArrayList internals. Option C and D are not applicable here.

options.text: Selection sort — finds minimum and swaps it to position i each iteration
Insertion sort — shifts elements right to insert arr[i] in sorted portion
Bubble sort — repeatedly swaps adjacent elements if out of order, largest bubbles to the end
Merge sort — divides array in half recursively and merges

questions.explanation: The outer loop runs n times; the inner loop shrinks from n-i-1 each iteration, swapping adjacent pairs if out of order. This is the classic bubble sort: the largest unsorted element 'bubbles up' to the end in each pass. Option A (selection sort) scans for the minimum and makes one swap per outer iteration. Option B (insertion sort) builds a sorted prefix by inserting each element into its correct position. Option D (merge sort) uses divide-and-conquer, not nested loops with swapping. After n-1 passes the array is sorted ascending.

questions.questionText: What is a stack data structure's LIFO property, and what is its most common use?

options.text: Last-In-First-Out — the most recently pushed element is popped first; used for DFS traversal, function call stack, undo mechanisms, and expression parsing
Last-In-First-Out — the first pushed element is popped first; used for BFS traversal and task queues
First-In-First-Out — used for breadth-first search and scheduling
LIFO only applies to linked lists; arrays use FIFO ordering

questions.explanation: LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) means the top of the stack (most recent push) is what gets popped next. This models real-world stacks like a pile of plates. Common uses: function call stack (recursion depth), undo/redo (most recent action undone first), matching brackets and expression evaluation (shunting-yard algorithm), and DFS graph traversal. Option B reverses LIFO. Option C describes a queue, not a stack. Option D is false.

questions.questionText: What is a hash table collision, and what is one common resolution strategy?

options.text: A collision occurs when two distinct keys produce the same hash code; chaining stores all colliding entries in a linked list at the bucket
A collision is when two threads access the hash table simultaneously; it requires locking to resolve
Collisions only occur with string keys; numeric keys never collide
The only resolution strategy is to rehash the entire table with a new hash function

questions.explanation: A collision is when hash(k1) == hash(k2) for distinct keys. The two main strategies are chaining (linked list or balanced tree at each bucket, O(n) worst-case but O(1) average) and open addressing / probing (linear/quadratic probing or double hashing, where colliding entries are placed in nearby slots). Load factor (n/m) and rehashing thresholds govern when to grow the table. Option B describes thread safety, not collision resolution. Option C is false (any hash function can produce collisions regardless of key type). Option D is too narrow — rehashing with a new function is one approach, but not the only one.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array of n elements?

options.text: O(log n) — the search space is halved with each comparison
O(n) — the array must be scanned in the worst case
O(1) — binary search accesses the middle element directly
O(n log n) — binary search requires log n passes over n elements

questions.explanation: Binary search repeatedly halves the search space: mid = (lo + hi) / 2, compare, then recurse left or right. After k steps, the remaining space is n/2^k, reduced to size 1 when 2^k ≥ n → k = ⌈log₂ n⌉. This is why sorted arrays enable O(log n) search vs O(n) linear search. Binary search requires random access (indexed array or equivalent), making it unsuitable for linked lists. Option C is wrong (the comparison takes O(1), not the whole algorithm). Option D miscounts — it's O(log n) total comparisons, not O(n log n).

questions.questionText: In a binary search tree (BST), what is the time complexity of searching, inserting, and finding the minimum element?

options.text: All operations are O(1) because BSTs use a hash-based lookup internally
Average case O(log n) for balanced trees; worst case O(n) for skewed trees (degenerate chain)
All operations are O(n) because the tree must be traversed completely
Search is O(n); insert is O(log n) — insertion only checks one path

questions.explanation: BST operations traverse a single path from root to a leaf (not the whole tree), giving O(h) where h is the height. For a balanced BST (self-balancing like AVL or Red-Black), h ≈ log₂ n → O(log n). For a skewed/degenerate BST (sorted insertion order), h = n → O(n). Finding minimum is O(h) — traverse left children until null. This is why self-balancing trees (AVL, Red-Black, B-trees) are used in databases: they maintain near-logarithmic height. Option A is false (BST ≠ hash table). Option C is false for balanced trees. Option D reverses search and insert.

questions.questionText: What is dynamic programming, and which classic problem does it solve optimally using memoization?

options.text: DP is a divide-and-conquer approach where subproblems are solved independently; it solves the Fibonacci sequence by computing F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) without caching
DP trades memory for time by storing results of overlapping subproblems to avoid recomputation; the naive recursive Fibonacci (exponential) becomes O(n) with memoization
DP is a greedy algorithm that always picks the locally optimal subproblem choice; it solves the knapsack problem optimally
DP only applies to problems with no overlapping subproblems, making it equivalent to divide-and-conquer

questions.explanation: DP applies when subproblems overlap (same subproblem solved multiple times). Memoization (top-down, caching recursive calls) or tabulation (bottom-up, filling a table) eliminates redundant computation. Fibonacci naive recursive is O(2^n) — exponential because it recomputes F(k) many times. With memoization, each F(k) is computed once → O(n) time, O(n) space. Option A describes memoized DP but omits the key distinction from naive recursion. Option C confuses DP with greedy (knapsack's fractional version is greedy; 0/1 knapsack is DP). Option D reverses the definition.

questions.questionText: What is Dijkstra's algorithm, and what is its time complexity with a binary min-heap priority queue?

options.text: Dijkstra finds shortest paths from one source to all vertices in a graph with negative edge weights; complexity is O(V log V + E)
Dijkstra finds shortest paths from a source to all vertices in a non-negative weighted graph; with a binary heap, complexity is O((V+E) log V)
Dijkstra uses BFS to find shortest paths in unweighted graphs; complexity is O(V+E)
Dijkstra solves the all-pairs shortest path problem using dynamic programming; complexity is O(V³ log V)

questions.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm computes shortest paths from a single source to all reachable vertices in graphs with non-negative edge weights. Using a binary min-heap (extract-min in O(log V), decrease-key in O(log V)), each edge relaxation costs O(log V) → total O(E log V) with O((V+E) log V) accounting for V extract-mins. With a Fibonacci heap, extract-min is O(1) amortized, giving O(E + V log V). Option A is wrong (negative weights break Dijkstra's greedy choice property). Option C describes BFS, not Dijkstra. Option D describes Floyd-Warshall (all-pairs, DP, O(V³)).

questions.questionText: What is a Trie (prefix tree) and what operations does it support efficiently?

options.text: A Trie is a binary search tree optimized for numeric keys; search, insert, and delete are all O(log n)
A Trie is a tree where each node stores a character and edges represent possible next characters; it supports O(m) search, insert, and prefix matching where m is the key length
A Trie is a hash table variant that uses prefix compression to reduce memory usage; operations are O(1) average
A Trie is equivalent to a B-tree with a branching factor equal to the alphabet size; it is used for sorting

questions.explanation: A Trie (from retrieval) stores strings by paths: each node is a character, edges are characters, and a flag marks word ends. Operations are O(m) where m is the string length (not n, the number of stored keys) — independent of dictionary size. This makes prefix/autocomplete (find all words starting with a prefix), longest prefix matching (IP routing), and spelling correction extremely efficient. Space is O(Σ m × alphabet size), which can be high for large alphabets (use compressed/ternary search trees to mitigate). Option A describes a BST, not Trie. Option C is wrong (Trie ≠ hash table). Option D confuses Tries with B-trees.

questions.questionText: What is the longest increasing subsequence (LIS) problem, and what is an optimal DP approach to solve it?

options.text: LIS finds the shortest contiguous subarray with the maximum sum using Kadane's algorithm in O(n)
LIS finds the longest non-contiguous subsequence where elements are in strictly increasing order; DP solution is O(n²) using dp[i] = max(dp[j] + 1) for all j < i where arr[j] < arr[i]
LIS finds all sorted permutations of the array using bit manipulation in O(n log n)
LIS can only be solved using a greedy algorithm that picks the smallest available element at each step

questions.explanation: The LIS problem: given an array, find the longest subsequence (not necessarily contiguous) where elements are in increasing order. The O(n²) DP solution computes dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) for all j < i where arr[j] < arr[i]. The overall LIS is max(dp[i]). The O(n log n) solution uses binary search with a tails array — for each element, find the first index ≥ itself in tails and replace (or append), maintaining a sorted list of minimal ending values. Option A describes maximum subarray sum (Kadane), not LIS. Option C is false. Option D is wrong (greedy fails for LIS — consider [3, 1, 2] where greedy picks 3 first, missing 1, 2).

questions.questionText: What is the primary difference between a singly linked list and a doubly linked list?

options.text: A singly linked list stores only a next pointer per node; a doubly linked list stores both next and prev pointers, enabling O(1) backward traversal and O(1) deletion given a node reference
A singly linked list has O(n) search; a doubly linked list has O(1) search due to bidirectional pointers
A doubly linked list uses less memory per node because it stores only one pointer instead of two
A singly linked list cannot implement a stack; a doubly linked list can

questions.explanation: Singly linked list: each node has .next pointer only → O(1) insertion at known position, O(n) search, O(n) deletion (requires finding previous node). Doubly linked list: each node has .next and .prev → enables O(1) backward traversal, O(1) deletion given a node reference (no need to find previous), and makes it ideal for LRU caches and browser back/forward navigation. Option B is false (both have O(n) search). Option C reverses the memory trade-off. Option D is false.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of linear search on an unsorted array of n elements in the worst case?

options.text: O(1) — the first element is checked immediately
O(log n) — the search space is halved each comparison
O(n) — every element may need to be checked before finding or concluding the target is absent
O(n²) — nested loops are required to compare every pair

questions.explanation: Linear search examines each element sequentially until either the target is found or the end of the array is reached. In the worst case (target absent or at the last position), all n elements are checked → O(n). Option A is the best case. Option B is binary search (requires sorted array). Option D describes comparison sorts, not search.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of searching in a max-heap?

options.text: O(1) — the max element is always at the root
O(log n) — the heap property allows binary search on the array representation
O(n) — a max-heap has no ordering that enables efficient search; all elements must be checked in the worst case
O(n log n) — heap sort is required to extract elements in sorted order

questions.explanation: A max-heap guarantees that the parent is ≥ its children, but it does NOT guarantee any ordering between left and right subtrees. This means you cannot search efficiently — the heap property only tells you the maximum is at the root. To find a value, you must scan the array, giving O(n) worst-case search. The heap property only guarantees O(log n) extract-max (pop root, heapify), not O(log n) search. Option A is correct for finding the maximum only. Option B misapplies the heap property. Option D describes sorting, not searching.

questions.questionText: Which sorting algorithm has O(n²) worst-case time complexity? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Bubble sort
Selection sort
Insertion sort
Merge sort

questions.explanation: Bubble sort (O(n²) worst/avg), Selection sort (O(n²) regardless of input order — always scans for min), and Insertion sort (O(n²) worst case — array in reverse sorted order) all have O(n²) worst-case complexity. Merge sort has O(n log n) worst-case guaranteed. Quick sort degrades to O(n²) in the worst case (unbalanced partitions). Selection sort is always O(n²) with no best-case improvement, making it the least adaptive of the three O(n²) sorts.

questions.questionText: Which operations on a queue data structure are O(1)? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Enqueue at the rear (with tail pointer)
Dequeue from the front (with head pointer)
Peek at the front element
Searching for a value by index

questions.explanation: Enqueue (push to rear with tail pointer) is O(1). Dequeue (pop from front with head pointer) is O(1). Peek (examine front without removing) is O(1). Searching by index requires traversal → O(n). In array-backed queues without a tail pointer, enqueue would be O(1) but you'd need head pointer for O(1) dequeue. With a circular buffer using both head and tail pointers, both enqueue and dequeue are O(1).

questions.questionText: A recursion tree for Fibonacci F(n) has exponential size. True or false?
questions.explanation: The naive Fibonacci recursion tree has approximately φ^n nodes (where φ ≈ 1.618, the golden ratio), which is exponential. This is because F(n) calls F(n-1) and F(n-2), and the same subproblems (like F(3)) are recomputed exponentially many times. This exponential redundancy is the motivation for dynamic programming — memoization or tabulation reduces it to O(n) by computing each value once.

questions.questionText: The space complexity of an iterative algorithm is always better than its recursive counterpart. True or false?
questions.explanation: Not always — it depends on the algorithm. The iterative version of Fibonacci uses O(1) space, but the recursive version with memoization uses O(n) space (memo table). However, the recursive version without memoization uses O(n) call stack depth but O(1) space for the memo table, which is different from O(n) stack. The statement is too broad — recursive depth vs iterative counters don't always favor one over the other.

questions.questionText: A binary tree with 3 levels can have at most how many nodes?

options.text: 6 nodes — 2 × 3 = 6
7 nodes — levels 0, 1, 2 (root to 2 edges) fully filled: 1 + 2 + 4 = 7
8 nodes — 2³ = 8
15 nodes — 2⁴ - 1 = 15

questions.explanation: With 3 levels (level 0 = root, level 1, level 2), the height is 2 edges (root to deepest leaf). Maximum nodes = 2^(h+1) - 1 = 2^3 - 1 = 7. Level 0: 2⁰=1, Level 1: 2¹=2, Level 2: 2²=4 → total 7. Option C (8) would be 4 levels. Option D (15) would be 4 levels of edges (5 levels of nodes).

questions.questionText: What is the correct order of average time complexities from fastest to slowest?

options.text: O(1) → O(log n) → O(n) → O(n log n) → O(n²)
O(1) → O(n) → O(log n) → O(n²) → O(n log n)
O(log n) → O(1) → O(n) → O(n²) → O(n log n)
O(n) → O(1) → O(log n) → O(n log n) → O(n²)

questions.explanation: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) for growing n. O(log n) is faster than O(n) but slower than O(1). O(n log n) is faster than O(n²) but slower than O(n). This ordering is used to compare algorithm efficiency: hash table lookup (O(1)) < binary search (O(log n)) < linear search (O(n)) < merge sort (O(n log n)) < bubble sort (O(n²)).

questions.questionText:
What is the output of the following algorithm?

arr = [5, 3, 8, 1, 2]
arr.sort()
print(arr)
questions.explanation: The sort() method (Python's Timsort) sorts the array in ascending order in-place. Result: [1, 2, 3, 5, 8]. Timsort is a hybrid stable sorting algorithm derived from merge sort and insertion sort, running in O(n log n) average case and O(n) best case (already sorted). Option A is descending sort (arr.sort(reverse=True)). Option C is wrong sorting behavior. Option D is an intermediate state.

questions.questionText: A B-tree of order m can have at most m children per node and m-1 keys. True or false?
questions.explanation: B-tree order m (or minimum degree t) defines the branching factor: maximum children = m, maximum keys = m-1. Minimum children = ceil(m/2) except root. B-trees are used in databases and file systems because their wide, shallow structure minimizes disk I/O — reading a node with m children may retrieve multiple keys in one disk access. The 'order' convention varies: some texts define order as maximum degree, others as minimum degree; the key property is that B-trees maintain balance with fan-out proportional to block size.

questions.questionText: What is the key difference between BFS and DFS in terms of data structure used?

options.text: BFS uses a stack (LIFO) and explores the deepest node first; DFS uses a queue (FIFO) and explores level by level
BFS uses a queue (FIFO) and explores nodes level by level; DFS uses a stack (LIFO) or recursion and explores depth-first
BFS and DFS both use a queue; only the traversal order differs
BFS and DFS both use a stack; only the traversal order differs

questions.explanation: BFS (Breadth-First Search) uses a queue — enqueue neighbors, dequeue and explore, giving FIFO (level-by-level) order. DFS (Depth-First Search) uses a stack (explicit or call stack via recursion) — push neighbors, pop and explore, giving LIFO (depth-first) order. Both visit each vertex once → O(V+E) time. BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs; DFS is used for topological sort, cycle detection, and finding connected components.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of merge sort, and what recurrence relation describes it?

options.text: O(n log n); T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) — insertion sort style
O(n log n); T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) — divides array in half, sorts each half, merges in linear time
O(n²); T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n²) — partitioning is quadratic
O(n); T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) — binary search style

questions.explanation: Merge sort: divide array into two halves (free operation), recursively sort each half, merge them (linear scan of both halves). Recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n). By Master theorem (case 2): a=2, b=2, d=1 → n^(log_b a) = n → T(n) = Θ(n log n). This is the textbook Master theorem example. Option A is insertion sort. Option D is binary search. Option C is nonsense.

questions.questionText: What is the recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1) and what does it describe?

options.text: Binary search — halves the problem each step
Merge sort — divides into two equal halves plus O(n) merge
Naive Fibonacci — each call branches into two, leading to exponential T(n) ≈ φ^n where φ ≈ 1.618
Selection sort — scans for minimum and recurses on the rest

questions.explanation: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1) describes the naive Fibonacci recursion. Each call makes two recursive calls (n-1 and n-2) plus O(1) work. This recurrence solves to exponential time T(n) ≈ φ^n ≈ 1.618^n because both branches are nearly full-size. The +1 work at each level compounds to give the golden ratio φ as the branching factor. This is why memoization transforms it to O(n) — converting two recursive calls into one table lookup each.

questions.questionText: Which of the following algorithms are comparison-based sorts? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Quick sort
Merge sort
Counting sort
Heap sort

questions.explanation: Quick sort, merge sort, and heap sort are all comparison-based sorts — they determine relative order by comparing pairs of elements (a[i] < a[j]?). Counting sort is NOT comparison-based; it uses element values as array indices, achieving O(n+k) by exploiting bounded integer ranges. Radix sort is also non-comparison (processes digits/bits). Comparison sorts have a theoretical lower bound of Ω(n log n).

questions.questionText: Which graph traversal algorithm is best suited for finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph?

options.text: DFS — explores as deep as possible before backtracking, guaranteeing shortest path
BFS — discovers all vertices at distance d before those at distance d+1, giving shortest path by edge count
Dijkstra — required even for unweighted graphs to find shortest path
Topological sort — sorts vertices so that all edges point forward, giving shortest path

questions.explanation: In an unweighted graph, BFS guarantees shortest path by number of edges because it explores level by level — all vertices at distance k are discovered before any at distance k+1. When the target is first reached, you've found a shortest path. DFS does not guarantee shortest path (it may find a longer path first). Dijkstra is needed only when edge weights exist and can be negative (use Bellman-Ford). Topological sort finds linear ordering for DAGs, not shortest paths.

Linear scan finds maximum: start with 3, update → 4, update → 5, update → 9 (largest), rest are ≤ 9. Result: 9. This is O(n) time, O(1) space — the standard maximum-finding algorithm. Option A (6) misses the 9. Option B (3) is the first element only. Option D (2) is wrong.

questions.questionText: What is the space complexity of merge sort?

options.text: O(1) — merge sort can be implemented in-place
O(n) — the merge step requires a temporary array proportional to the subarray being merged
O(log n) — only recursion depth requires logarithmic space
O(n log n) — every level requires a new auxiliary array

questions.explanation: Merge sort's merge step needs a temporary array of size proportional to the combined subarray → O(n) space. Recursion depth is O(log n). Total: O(n) auxiliary space (or O(n + log n) = O(n)). Some in-place variants exist but are complex. Quick sort is O(log n) space (recursion stack), making it preferable for memory-constrained environments. Heap sort is O(1) — fully in-place.

questions.questionText: Which problems does the Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) data structure solve efficiently? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Detecting whether two elements are in the same connected component
Finding the shortest path in a weighted graph
Detecting cycles in a graph
Merging two sets of elements into one

questions.explanation: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) supports two operations: Find (which set does an element belong to?) and Union (merge two sets). It efficiently solves: (1) connected components — are two nodes connected? (Kruskal's MST), (2) cycle detection in undirected graphs (if Find(u) == Find(v) before adding edge u-v, a cycle exists), (3) dynamic connectivity. Union-Find with path compression and union by rank achieves near O(1) amortized per operation (inverse Ackermann α(n)). Shortest path is solved by Dijkstra or Bellman-Ford.

questions.questionText: What is the lower bound Ω(n log n) for comparison sorts based on?

options.text: There are n! possible permutations and each comparison eliminates at most half, requiring Ω(log₂(n!)) ≈ Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case
Every comparison sort must examine every element at least once, taking Ω(n) time
The best sorting algorithms use binary search, which has O(log n) complexity, and sorting requires log n searches per element
The Master theorem case 1 proves that T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) has Ω(n log n) lower bound

questions.explanation: Comparison sorts model sorting as a binary decision tree: each comparison (a[i] < a[j]?) has 2 outcomes. To distinguish all n! possible permutations, the tree must have ≥ n! leaves. A binary tree with L leaves has height ≥ log₂ L. Minimum height = log₂(n!) ≈ n log₂ n - n log₂ e (Stirling's approximation). Therefore no comparison sort can beat Ω(n log n) in the worst case. This lower bound is tight — merge sort and heap sort achieve Θ(n log n). Non-comparison sorts (counting, radix) can beat this bound under special conditions.

questions.questionText: A recursive algorithm that calls itself 3 times at each level with problem size n/3, and does O(n) work at each level, has what time complexity?

options.text: O(n log 3) — linearithmic with branching factor 3
O(n^(log_3 3)) = O(n) — the exponent cancels the recursion depth
O(n^(log_3 3)) = O(n) — incorrect use of Master theorem
T(n) = 3T(n/3) + O(n) → O(n log n) by Master theorem case 2 (a=b^d)

questions.explanation: Master theorem: T(n) = aT(n/b) + O(n^d). Here a=3, b=3, d=1. n^(log_b a) = n^(log_3 3) = n^1 = n. f(n) = Θ(n) = Θ(n^1). Case 2 (f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a))): T(n) = Θ(n^d log n) = Θ(n log n). The work at each level (O(n)) times the number of levels (log_3 n) = O(n log n). Option A misapplies the branching factor. Option B misidentifies the recurrence. Option C is wrong.

questions.questionText: A red-black tree guarantees O(log n) time for search, insert, and delete. True or false?
questions.explanation: Red-Black trees are self-balancing BSTs with these guarantees: (1) height ≤ 2*log₂(n+1), so the longest root-to-leaf path is at most twice the shortest. (2) All three operations (search, insert, delete) traverse one path from root to leaf → O(log n). Insert and delete involve O(log n) rotations/recoloring at most. Used in C++ STL (map/set), Java TreeMap/TreeSet, and the Linux kernel CFS scheduler. AVL trees have stricter balance (height difference ≤ 1) and thus faster search but slower insert/delete.

questions.questionText: A priority queue implemented with a binary heap has O(log n) insert and O(log n) extract-min. True or false?
questions.explanation: Binary min-heap: insert adds at the end (last level, rightmost position) and bubbles up (O(log n) — height of tree). Extract-min removes root, replaces with last element, bubbles down (O(log n)). Heapify (building a heap from unsorted array) runs in O(n) via the bottom-up Floyd algorithm, which is faster than inserting n elements one by one O(n log n). A max-heap reverses min/max for max priority queue.

questions.questionText:
What is the output of the following algorithm?

def bit_count(n):
    count = 0
    while n:
        n &= (n - 1)
        count += 1
    return count

print(bit_count(14))
questions.explanation: n &= (n-1) clears the lowest set bit in one operation. 14 in binary = 1110. Iteration 1: n=1110 & 1101=1100 (bits left: 3), iteration 2: n=1100 & 1011=1000 (bits left: 2), iteration 3: n=1000 & 0111=0000 (bits left: 1). Total: 3 set bits. This is Brian Kernighan's algorithm for counting set bits — each iteration removes one set bit, so the loop runs exactly once per set bit → O(number of set bits). Option A is 1110=14. Option B is 1000=8. Option D is wrong.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of the Bellman-Ford algorithm for shortest paths from a single source in a graph with V vertices and E edges?

options.text: O(V + E) — single-pass BFS-style relaxation
O(V log V + E log V) — Dijkstra with a binary heap
O(V×E) — relaxes all edges V-1 times; detects negative weight cycles on V-th pass
O(V³) — all-pairs shortest path, computed for each source

questions.explanation: Bellman-Ford: relax all edges V-1 times. Each relaxation pass scans all E edges → O(V×E). After V-1 passes, if any edge can still be relaxed, a negative weight cycle exists (detectable in O(E)). This is slower than Dijkstra O(V log V + E) for non-negative weights, but Bellman-Ford handles negative weights and detects negative cycles. Optimizations: if a pass makes no changes, exit early. Option A is BFS. Option B is Dijkstra with heap. Option D is Floyd-Warshall.

questions.questionText: What is the Floyd-Warshall algorithm and what is its time and space complexity?

options.text: Floyd-Warshall finds shortest paths from one source to all vertices; O(V³) time, O(V²) space
Floyd-Warshall finds all-pairs shortest paths using dynamic programming on intermediate vertices; O(V³) time, O(V²) space
Floyd-Warshall is equivalent to running Dijkstra V times; O(V×E log V) time
Floyd-Warshall finds minimum spanning trees; O(V³) time, O(V²) space

questions.explanation: Floyd-Warshall DP: dp[k][i][j] = shortest path from i to j using only vertices {1..k} as intermediate. Transition: dp[k][i][j] = min(dp[k-1][i][j], dp[k-1][i][k] + dp[k-1][k][j]). Space-optimized to 2D matrix (V×V). Time: O(V³) — triple nested loop. Works with negative weights (no negative cycles) and finds all-pairs shortest paths simultaneously. For sparse graphs, running Dijkstra V times (O(V×E log V)) is faster. For dense graphs (E ≈ V²), Floyd-Warshall is competitive. Option A is wrong (not single-source). Option C is wrong. Option D is wrong (MST uses Prim/Kruskal).

questions.questionText: What is A* search, and what is its optimality condition?

options.text: A* is a graph traversal algorithm equivalent to BFS with additional pruning; it is always optimal

A* uses f(n) = g(n) + h(n) where g(n) is cost from start and h(n) is heuristic estimate to goal; it finds the optimal path if the heuristic is admissible (never overestimates) and consistent (satisfies triangle inequality)
A* is a greedy algorithm that always picks the node with smallest h(n); it finds shortest path in O(V log V)
A* is equivalent to Dijkstra with a heuristic equal to 0; it always runs faster than Dijkstra

questions.explanation: A* combines path cost g(n) (like Dijkstra) and heuristic h(n) (like greedy best-first): f(n) = g(n) + h(n). The priority queue orders by f(n). Optimality: if h(n) is admissible (never overestimates true cost to goal) AND consistent (h(n) satisfies h(n) ≤ cost(n→n') + h(n') for all edges), A* returns the optimal path. If h(n)=0 for all nodes, A* reduces to Dijkstra. If h(n) = exact distance to goal, A* finds the goal without exploring anything else. The more accurate h(n), the fewer nodes A* expands.

questions.questionText: Which of the following DP problems exhibit optimal substructure AND overlapping subproblems? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Fibonacci sequence
Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)
Computing n!
0/1 Knapsack

questions.explanation: Fibonacci: F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) — same subproblems recur exponentially many times in naive recursion. LCS: optimal solution uses optimal solutions of prefixes — subproblems overlap in the DP table (dp[i][j] depends on dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). 0/1 Knapsack: optimal solution for capacity w using items 1..n uses optimal solutions for subcapabilities (optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems table). n! has optimal substructure but no overlapping subproblems — each subproblem is computed exactly once, making memoization irrelevant.

questions.questionText: What is bit manipulation used for, and what is the result of 0b1101 & 0b1011?

options.text: Bit manipulation manipulates individual bits for speed; 1101 & 1011 = 0b1001 (9)
Bit manipulation manipulates individual bits for speed; 1101 & 1011 = 0b1001 (9)
Bit manipulation is a sorting technique; 1101 & 1011 = 0b1101
Bit manipulation only works on floating-point numbers; 1101 & 1011 is undefined

questions.explanation:
AND: bitwise AND. 1101 & 1011:
  1101
& 1011
= 1001
0b1001 = 9. Bitwise operations (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, shifts) are used for flags, masks, subset enumeration, optimized arithmetic (n & (n-1) clears lowest bit), and set operations. XOR trick: a ^ a = 0, a ^ 0 = a. Shifts: << doubles, >> halves (unsigned). These are O(1) per operation.

questions.questionText:
What is the output of the following algorithm?

def power_of_two(n):
    return n > 0 and (n & (n - 1)) == 0

print(power_of_two(8), power_of_two(6), power_of_two(0))
questions.explanation: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit. For a power of two (8=0b1000), n-1=7=0b0111 → 0b1000 & 0b0111 = 0. For 6=0b0110, n-1=5=0b0101 → 0b0110 & 0b0101 = 0b0100 ≠ 0. So power_of_two(8)=True, power_of_two(6)=False. power_of_two(0): n>0 is False → short-circuit returns False. Result: True, False, False. This is the classic bit trick for checking powers of two.

questions.questionText: What is the rod cutting problem, and how does dynamic programming solve it?

options.text: A rod of length n must be cut into pieces of fixed lengths; greedy always picks the longest piece fitting — O(n) time
Given prices p[i] for length i, find max revenue by cutting a rod of length n into pieces; dp[i] = max(dp[i], p[j] + dp[i-j]) for all cut lengths j, giving O(n²) time
A rod must be cut without wasting any material; exact cover algorithms solve it in O(2^n)
Rod cutting is equivalent to the activity selection problem; greedy by shortest cut length is optimal

questions.explanation: Rod cutting: given prices p[1..n] for pieces of length 1..n, maximize revenue by cutting. Let revenue[i] = max revenue for length i. For each i, try all possible first cuts j (0 to i): revenue[i] = max(p[j] + revenue[i-j]). dp[0] = 0. This is O(n²) time, O(n) space. Equivalent to 0/1 knapsack (each cut can be taken at most once, lengths/values). Without DP, this is exponential (2^(n-1) possible cut combinations). The classic example: length 4 with prices p[1]=1,p[2]=5,p[3]=6,p[4]=9 → optimal: cut into 2+2 for revenue 10.

questions.questionText: The time complexity of the A* algorithm with an admissible heuristic is O(b^d) where b is branching factor and d is solution depth. True or false?
questions.explanation: With an admissible (never overestimates) heuristic, A* is optimally efficient — it expands the minimum possible number of nodes to guarantee finding the optimal solution. In the worst case, A* expands O(b^d) nodes where b is the branching factor and d is the solution depth. Without heuristics (b=branching factor, purely exhaustive), all V nodes are visited. With a perfect heuristic, A* expands only the nodes on the optimal path. In practice, heuristic quality determines how much search is pruned. This exponential bound is why A* is not feasible for very deep search spaces without strong heuristics.

questions.questionText: The divide-and-conquer technique always produces O(n log n) algorithms. True or false?
questions.explanation: Divide-and-conquer (D&C) splits a problem into subproblems, solves them recursively, and combines results. The time complexity depends on how the subproblems divide and the combine cost. Merge sort: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n log n). Binary search: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) → O(log n). Strassen's matrix multiplication: T(n) = 7T(n/2) + O(n²) → O(n^(log₂7) ≈ O(n^2.807). Karatsuba multiplication: T(n) = 3T(n/2) + O(n) → O(n^(log₂3) ≈ O(n^1.585). The 'combine' cost determines the exponent, not D&C itself.
